application_angle: The framework can guide practical fiber-sensor placement for smart-city monitoring while clarifying when privacy-sensitive tracking becomes possible.

title: Projected logical ensembles in surface codes via the random-matrix theory of quantum dots
abstract: Measurements underpin active quantum error correction (QEC) and have been recognized as a source of novel measurement-induced many-body phenomena. Here, we study the statistical properties of post-measurement logical states arising in QEC on topological codes subject to deterministic transversal unitary gates. Upon syndrome extraction followed by maximum-likelihood decoding, a Born-weighted ensemble arises which we dub the "projected logical ensemble" (PLE). Focusing on surface codes subject to uniform single-qubit Pauli-$X$ rotations, we characterize the measurement-induced randomness of the PLE. To this end, we show that for a code with a single logical qubit, the PLE is isomorphic to an ensemble of scattering matrices describing mesoscopic quantum dots obtained from a 2D Majorana network model with suitable boundary conditions. We uncover regimes where these quantum dots are chaotic such that their scattering matrices are well-described by random matrix theory. In these regimes, the PLE approaches a universal ensemble that is maximally random up to symmetry and decoder-induced constraints. The symmetry constraints, set by stabilizer and logical operator weights, realize Altland-Zirnbauer classes D or DIII, which we both illustrate. Our results establish a fundamental connection between emergent universality concepts in mesoscopic physics, quantum many-body systems, and QEC.
one_liner: Surface-code post-measurement logical states are mapped to random-matrix ensembles from mesoscopic quantum-dot theory.
why_it_matters: It connects QEC measurement randomness to universal statistical physics, informing how decoding and gates shape logical-state ensembles.
detailed_summary: The paper defines projected logical ensembles arising after syndrome extraction and maximum-likelihood decoding in surface codes under transversal Pauli-X rotations. For a single logical qubit, the ensemble is shown to be isomorphic to scattering matrices of mesoscopic quantum dots built from a 2D Majorana network model. In chaotic regimes, the post-measurement logical ensemble approaches universal random-matrix behavior constrained by symmetries and the decoder. The relevant Altland-Zirnbauer classes include D and DIII, set by stabilizer and logical-operator weights.
main_result: The main result is an exact mapping between decoded surface-code logical ensembles and quantum-dot scattering-matrix ensembles with universal chaotic regimes.
application_angle: Understanding these statistical structures can help characterize logical randomness and decoder-induced constraints in fault-tolerant quantum hardware.

title: When Renormalisation Remembers: UV/IR Mixing as an Entanglement Bridge
abstract: Renormalisation is traditionally understood to be a Wilsonian memoryless process in which ultraviolet (UV) degrees of freedom gradually decouple, leaving an autonomous infrared (IR) description. However this need not be the case: in UV/IR mixed theories correlations between widely separated scales can persist. In this work I recast UV/IR mixing as a Hilbert-space phenomenon, realised as correlations across renormalisation scales. This formulation is implemented using the Born-Reciprocal Tensor Network (BRTN), a new configuration of tensor network that is globally symmetric under phase-space reciprocity. On this network I prepare the vacuum and reproduce the expected radiative corrections. The resulting renormalisation geometry exhibits memory, with a bridge linking reciprocal representations of IR physics, whose cross-bridge entanglement provides a precise criterion for the viability of an effective description. I analyse when this criterion is met, and show that there is a large-volume limit, with the fundamental scale held fixed, in which the obstruction to a local description scales away: Wilsonian behaviour is restored and renormalisation forgets. The BRTN therefore provides a concrete and calculable platform for UV/IR mixing.
one_liner: A Born-reciprocal tensor network recasts UV/IR mixing as entanglement across renormalisation scales.
why_it_matters: It provides a calculable tensor-network criterion for when effective low-energy descriptions retain or lose ultraviolet memory.
detailed_summary: The work formulates UV/IR mixing as Hilbert-space correlations across renormalisation scales using a Born-Reciprocal Tensor Network. The network is symmetric under phase-space reciprocity and reproduces expected vacuum radiative corrections. Its renormalisation geometry contains a memory bridge between reciprocal IR representations, with cross-bridge entanglement serving as a criterion for effective-description viability. In a large-volume limit at fixed fundamental scale, the obstruction scales away and Wilsonian behavior is restored.
main_result: The paper introduces BRTN as a concrete tensor-network platform where UV/IR mixing appears as scale-bridging entanglement.
application_angle: The framework may aid simulation and diagnosis of scale mixing in quantum field models, though its relevance is mainly theoretical rather than device-level.

title: Tripartite entanglement of remote atomic qubits
abstract: Distributed entanglement across multi-node quantum networks is essential for a wide range of quantum technologies, including modular quantum computers, distributed sensing and metrology, and multi-party secure communication protocols. Such large-scale quantum networks will require photonic interconnects to generate and sustain entangled states across localized nodes. Previously, three-node distributed Greenberger-Horne-Zeilinger (GHZ) states have been generated between solid-state qubits and atomic ensembles, but not yet in the platform of individual atomic qubits, which can be replicated, detected, and individually controlled with high fidelity. Here we report the first fully-distributed GHZ state of qubits across a three-node quantum network of single atomic memories, using photonic interconnects. We achieve a bounded fidelity of $0.841(17) \leq \mathcal{F} \leq 0.881(17)$ at an entanglement generation rate of 0.095(5)/sec and measure a clear violation of Mermin's inequality while closing the detection loophole for the first time in a fully-distributed multipartite entangled state.
one_liner: Three remote single-atom memories are entangled into a fully distributed GHZ state using photonic interconnects.
why_it_matters: It demonstrates a key primitive for multi-node quantum networks with individually controlled atomic qubits.
detailed_summary: The experiment generates tripartite entanglement across a three-node network of individual atomic memories connected by photons. The reported distributed GHZ-state fidelity is bounded between 0.841(17) and 0.881(17). The entanglement generation rate is 0.095(5) per second, and the authors observe a clear Mermin-inequality violation. They also close the detection loophole for a fully distributed multipartite entangled state.
main_result: The authors demonstrate the first fully distributed GHZ state across three remote individual atomic qubits with photonic links.
application_angle: This is directly relevant to modular quantum computing, distributed metrology, and multi-party quantum communication protocols.

title: Hybrid Ferromagnet-SNSPDs: Single photon induced order-to-disorder transition in ferromagnets coupled to thin film superconductors
abstract: The development of midwave and longwave infrared single photon detectors is crucial for their emerging applications in spectroscopy, remote sensing, exoplanet detection, and free space quantum communications. However, existing sensors need to be operated at extremely low temperatures (0.08-0.9K) to reduce dark noise and hence require the use of advanced cryogenics such as dilution refrigerators or $^3$He cryogens, significantly limiting applications. Here we propose a vortex-engineering approach based on a hybrid phase transition in a ferromagnet/superconductor bilayer to increase the operating temperature of infrared single photon detectors up to 3.75K. We show that the introduction of a ferromagnetic layer produces a local magnetic field which impedes vortex crossing in the superconductor, reducing dark noise. When a single photon is incident, the photon-induced hotspot causes an order-to-disorder transition in the ferromagnet, leading to a vortex-induced phase transition in the superconducting layer. By engineering the ferromagnet's Curie temperature to be close to the device's operating temperature, single photon sensitivity can be achieved at increased operating temperatures. We predict at midwave/longwave infrared wavelengths (3-14$μ$m) the operating temperature can be raised to 3.25-3.75K, enabling significantly simpler cooling systems.
one_liner: Hybrid ferromagnet-SNSPD design targets MWIR/LWIR single-photon detection at 3.25-3.75 K.
why_it_matters: Raising infrared SNSPD operating temperature could replace dilution or He-3 cooling with simpler cryocoolers for sensing and free-space quantum links.
detailed_summary: The paper proposes a ferromagnet/superconductor bilayer SNSPD in which the ferromagnetic layer suppresses dark-count vortex crossings via its local magnetic field. A photon hotspot disorders the ferromagnet near its Curie point, enabling a vortex-driven superconducting transition as the detection mechanism. Modeling predicts single-photon sensitivity across 3-14 um wavelengths at 3.25-3.75 K.
main_result: The proposed vortex-engineered detector is predicted to reach MWIR/LWIR single-photon sensitivity at 3.25-3.75 K.
application_angle: This directly targets infrared spectroscopy, remote sensing, exoplanet detection, and free-space quantum communication with less demanding cryogenics.

title: Post-Selection Probability and Fidelity of Bidirectional Teleportation
abstract: Understanding the scrambling of quantum information is central to many areas of quantum physics, including quantum thermalization, entanglement growth, and quantum information processing. Insights from these studies have, in turn, inspired the development of novel quantum protocols and algorithms. Recently, a bidirectional teleportation protocol was proposed to implement a digital SWAP operation between qubits by leveraging chaotic Hamiltonian evolution combined with measurement and post-selection. In this work, we provide a comprehensive study of two central quantities that characterize the protocol, the post-selection probability and the fidelity, taking into account possible errors in time-reversed dynamics. We show that these quantities can be expressed in terms of standard diagnostics in quantum dynamics, including the Loschmidt echo and its subsystem variant. The results unveil (1) the initial-state dependence of the fidelity and (2) the stability of the post-selection probability in integrable models. Our findings offer practical guidance for the implementation of the protocol on realistic quantum devices.
one_liner: Analyzes fidelity and post-selection probability for bidirectional teleportation under imperfect time reversal.
why_it_matters: It connects protocol performance to measurable chaos diagnostics, giving implementation guidance for teleportation-based SWAPs on realistic devices.
detailed_summary: The work studies a bidirectional teleportation protocol that implements a digital SWAP using chaotic evolution, measurement, and post-selection. It derives the post-selection probability and fidelity in terms of Loschmidt echo diagnostics, including subsystem variants, while incorporating time-reversal errors. The analysis highlights initial-state-dependent fidelity and unexpectedly stable post-selection probability in integrable models.
main_result: The paper expresses teleportation success probability and fidelity through standard quantum-dynamics diagnostics under realistic reversal errors.
application_angle: The results inform how to benchmark and deploy measurement-assisted teleportation primitives in quantum processors and communication-style protocols.

title: Effects of Josephson Junction Non-idealities on Adiabatic Quantum Flux Parametron Circuits
abstract: Adiabatic quantum flux parametron (AQFP) gate is a promising approach to scale up the cryogenic microwave electronics for superconducting qubit multiplexed control. However, the performance of these circuits depends on the quality of the Josephson junctions which are ideally superconductor-insulator-superconductor (SIS) type following the ideal sinusoidal relation between current and quantum phase. We demonstrate how the non-sinusoidal current-phase relation in Superconductor-Normal metal-Superconductor (SNS) and weak link (WL) junctions affects the speed, delay, and margin of the AQFP gates. The JJ models are defined in the Keysight ADS simulator using symbolically defined device (SDD) method.
one_liner: Simulates how non-ideal Josephson junction current-phase relations affect AQFP gate speed, delay, and margins.
why_it_matters: AQFP cryogenic logic is a candidate for scalable superconducting-qubit control, and junction non-idealities can set practical design limits.
detailed_summary: The paper examines adiabatic quantum flux parametron gates using non-sinusoidal current-phase relations from SNS and weak-link Josephson junctions rather than ideal SIS junctions. Device models are implemented in Keysight ADS with the symbolically defined device method. The study evaluates resulting changes in switching speed, delay, and operating margin for AQFP logic.
main_result: It quantifies how SNS and weak-link junction non-idealities modify AQFP gate timing and robustness metrics in circuit simulation.
application_angle: The findings guide junction technology choices for cryogenic microwave electronics used in multiplexed superconducting-qubit control.

title: Pulse-optimised circuit elements for scalable and noise-resilient quantum chemistry
abstract: Useful chemistry calculations on near-term quantum processors are hindered by current algorithmic runtimes. We develop a methodology to significantly reduce these runtimes. Typically, variational quantum eigensolver (VQE) algorithms are implemented as sequences of primitive gates. Our methodology instead relies on gradient-ascent pulse engineering to construct hardware-tailored pulses for the direct implementation of VQEs. As problem sizes increase, it quickly becomes intractable to optimise a pulse that implements an entire VQE ansatz circuit. However, leading VQEs are constructed in a modular fashion. A problem-tailored VQE is assembled from parameterised circuit elements that simulate hopping between two or four electronic spin orbitals. We show that these circuit elements can be implemented more efficiently using hardware-tailored pulses. We numerically demonstrate our methodology on a silicon spin-qubit quantum processor. We find that common circuit elements, known as single- and double-qubit excitations, can be implemented in less than 289 ns and 927 ns, respectively. Compared with conventional gate-based implementations, our pulse-accelerated qubit excitations provide a scalable approach for faster and therefore more noise-robust quantum chemistry simulations by reducing VQE runtimes by up to a factor of 15.3.
one_liner: Hardware-tailored pulses accelerate VQE excitation blocks for silicon spin-qubit quantum chemistry.
why_it_matters: Shorter chemistry circuits reduce noise exposure and make near-term molecular simulations more practical on constrained quantum hardware.
detailed_summary: The paper replaces primitive-gate decompositions of VQE building blocks with gradient-ascent-optimized control pulses tailored to a silicon spin-qubit processor. It focuses on modular single- and double-qubit excitation elements used in electronic-structure ansatze. Numerics show implementations below 289 ns and 927 ns, respectively, producing up to a 15.3x VQE runtime reduction versus gate-based circuits.
main_result: Pulse-optimized excitation blocks implement key VQE circuit elements up to 15.3x faster than conventional gate decompositions in simulation.
application_angle: This improves the feasibility of useful quantum chemistry workloads by lowering runtime and accumulated decoherence on near-term devices.

title: Canonical regularization of the stationary Coulomb problem and an Aufbau-like spectral ordering
abstract: The stationary hydrogen atom has Coulomb degeneracy across orbital levels, whereas the Aufbau/Madelung ordering is an empirical, many-electron rule established in atomic physics. We examine the hydrogen atom through a regularized de Broglie--Bohm representation, in which stationary amplitude current constraints generate separable Sturm--Liouville branches. In this formulation, the radial, orbital, and magnetic sectors acquire canonical Langer-like inverse square corrections. The modified boundary value problems allow analytical solutions and produce a hydrogen-like spectrum with regularized radial and angular indices. Consequently, radial Coulomb quantization acquires an orbital dependent shift, lifting the Coulomb degeneracy and producing a spectral ordering that follows the Aufbau/Madelung sequence. On this basis, we construct the ordering of the regularized de Broglie--Bohm states and show that the spectral structure retains the standard degenerate Rydberg sequence in the l=0 sector. The separated amplitudes are represented by generalized special function branches, including the associated Laguerre, Legendre, and Bessel functions with non-integral parameters arising from regularized separation. Therefore, the treatment is intended as an analytical examination of spectral ordering in a regularized one center Coulomb problem rather than as a replacement for the many electron atomic structure theory. Keywords: de Broglie--Bohm representation; Coulomb spectrum; canonical regularization; Langer correction; Sturm--Liouville equations; Aufbau principle; Madelung ordering; associated Legendre functions; associated Laguerre functions; Bessel functions.
one_liner: Regularized de Broglie-Bohm Coulomb equations yield an Aufbau-like ordering in a one-center spectrum.
why_it_matters: Although theoretical, it offers an analytic spectral-ordering framework that may inform quantum modeling methods rather than hardware directly.
detailed_summary: The paper formulates the stationary hydrogen atom in a regularized de Broglie-Bohm representation with amplitude-current constraints. Langer-like inverse-square corrections appear in radial, orbital, and magnetic sectors, yielding separable Sturm-Liouville branches with analytical special-function solutions. The resulting orbital-dependent radial shift lifts Coulomb degeneracy and produces an Aufbau/Madelung-like spectral ordering while preserving the standard Rydberg sequence for l=0.
main_result: The analysis derives a hydrogen-like spectrum whose regularized indices generate Aufbau-like ordering in a single-center problem.
application_angle: Its relevance is mainly methodological for analytic quantum models and spectral structure, with indirect value for simulation and theory toolchains.

title: Time-spectral control of accidental coincidences in daylight entanglement-based free-space QKD
abstract: Daylight entanglement-based free-space quantum key distribution (QKD) is limited by accidental coincidences from receiver-admitted background light. We develop and experimentally validate a receiver-level framework linking receiver bandwidth, accepted temporal width, and background-noise density to Bob singles, sifted-key rate, error rate, and quantum bit error rate (QBER) in telecom-wavelength BBM92 QKD. Indoor sweeps show that useful sifted counts saturate near the source-matched bandwidth, whereas broader bandwidth or higher background mainly increases accidental contamination. Increasing the accepted temporal width leaves Bob singles nearly unchanged but directly raises QBER by enlarging the random-overlap probability. A two-dimensional design map shows that the temporal-window margin contracts rapidly with increasing background-to-signal ratio, while the bandwidth margin remains comparatively broad near source-matched filtering. A 10 m rooftop daylight experiment demonstrates operation in the predicted low-accidental regime, yielding a mean sifted-key rate of 2,811 cps and a mean QBER of 4.43%.
one_liner: Receiver bandwidth and timing-window control suppress accidental coincidences in daylight entanglement-based free-space QKD.
why_it_matters: It gives experimentally validated design rules for keeping daylight QBER low in telecom-band entanglement QKD links.
detailed_summary: The paper develops a receiver-level model linking spectral bandwidth, accepted temporal width, and background density to Bob singles, sifted-key rate, error rate, and QBER. Indoor sweeps show useful sifted counts saturate near source-matched bandwidth, while extra bandwidth or wider time windows mainly increase accidental coincidences. A 10 m rooftop daylight test reaches 2,811 cps mean sifted-key rate with 4.43% mean QBER in the predicted low-accidental regime.
main_result: The experiment validates time-spectral receiver tuning for daylight entanglement QKD, achieving 2,811 cps sifted key rate and 4.43% QBER.
application_angle: The framework supports practical daylight free-space QKD receiver design where background light would otherwise dominate errors.

title: Broadband High-Level Squeezed Light using Waveguide Optical Parametric Amplifiers with External Dispersion Compensation
abstract: We demonstrate broadband phase-sensitive amplification (PSA) measurement of squeezed light generated by a waveguide optical parametric amplifier (OPA) with external dispersion compensation. In broadband systems, group velocity dispersion (GVD) induces a frequency-dependent rotation of the squeezing axis, which limits the observable bandwidth in PSA measurements. To overcome this limitation, we introduce external dispersion compensation between two OPAs and suppress the quadrature rotation over a wide frequency range. As a result, we observe a maximum squeezing of 5.9 dB near the carrier frequency and more than 5 dB of squeezing up to a frequency offset of 4.5 THz from the carrier. Furthermore, squeezing below the shot-noise level is confirmed up to a frequency offset of 6 THz from the carrier, corresponding to the accessible phase-matching bandwidth of the waveguide OPA. Our results establish a practical method for broadband characterization of squeezed light and provide a key step toward ultrafast continuous-variable quantum information processing.
one_liner: Waveguide OPAs with external dispersion compensation deliver multi-THz, high-level squeezed-light readout.
why_it_matters: Flattening quadrature rotation makes broadband continuous-variable resources measurable and usable for ultrafast quantum photonics.
detailed_summary: The work addresses group-velocity-dispersion-induced rotation of the squeezing axis, which otherwise narrows phase-sensitive amplification measurements in broadband waveguide OPAs. By inserting external dispersion compensation between two OPAs, the authors suppress quadrature rotation across the device bandwidth. They report 5.9 dB squeezing near the carrier, more than 5 dB out to 4.5 THz offset, and sub-shot-noise behavior to 6 THz.
main_result: External dispersion compensation enables observable broadband squeezing exceeding 5 dB across multi-THz offsets.
application_angle: The method supports practical characterization and deployment of ultrafast continuous-variable quantum information channels.

title: Impact of Network Constraints on Fault-Tolerant Distributed Quantum Computing
abstract: As we move towards scalable and modular quantum computing, quantum data centres become imperative. Existing analyses typically treat network constraints in isolation or through simplified models, leaving the interplay between error correction operations and communication resources underexplored. In this work, we present an end-to-end simulation framework that jointly models surface-code operations, internal QPU connectivity, and realistic network constraints including finite entanglement generation rates, limited communication qubits, and bandwidth contention, producing execution latency, from which logical error rate estimates are obtained. The framework is modular by design, allowing individual components such as routing heuristics, scheduling policies, and network topologies to be independently replaced. Numerical evaluation reveals distinct operating regimes in which the optimal resource allocation and code distance selection shift depending on the network characteristics. These results point to tradeoffs in the design of distributed quantum computing architectures that are not visible when computation and communication are modeled separately.
one_liner: End-to-end simulation links surface-code execution with realistic network bottlenecks in distributed quantum data centers.
why_it_matters: It exposes architecture tradeoffs in modular fault-tolerant quantum computers that are missed by compute-only or network-only models.
detailed_summary: The paper builds a modular simulator that jointly models surface-code operations, internal QPU connectivity, finite entanglement generation rates, communication-qubit limits, and bandwidth contention. It converts execution latency into logical error-rate estimates, allowing routing, scheduling, and topology choices to be swapped and compared. Numerical studies identify regimes where optimal code distance and resource allocation shift with network constraints.
main_result: The framework shows that communication bottlenecks can change optimal fault-tolerant resource allocation and code-distance choices.
application_angle: These results guide hardware and network co-design for scalable modular quantum computing systems.

title: Photon anti-bunching in high harmonic generation
abstract: Photon anti-bunching is the direct evidence for the existence of photons without having a classical counterpart. Unlike bunching of photons, which can have a semi-classical description, the effect of photon anti-bunching can only be understood with quantized electromagnetic fields. However, for the process of high harmonic generation (HHG), where many photons of the driving field are upconverted to a single photon of higher energy, there is yet no clear evidence for the presence of individual photon emission. The key result of this work is the prediction of photon anti-bunching in the process of HHG, marking it the first theoretical discovery of non-classicality in the temporal correlations of HHG photons. While other non-classical signatures in HHG, such as sub-Poissonian statistics or squeezing, have been discussed for an ensemble of photons, the anti-bunching signature reported here is a signature of a single photon. This is achieved by using the recently developed Heisenberg picture approach for quantum optical HHG, revealing clear anti-bunching signatures in the intensity correlation function across the entire harmonic spectrum.
one_liner: A quantum-optical theory predicts photon anti-bunching across high-harmonic spectra.
why_it_matters: Anti-bunching would be a direct single-photon nonclassical signature in HHG, beyond ensemble squeezing or sub-Poissonian statistics.
detailed_summary: Using a Heisenberg-picture quantum-optical treatment of high harmonic generation, the authors compute temporal intensity correlations of emitted harmonic photons. The analysis predicts anti-bunching throughout the harmonic spectrum, indicating individual photon emission in a process often modeled semiclassically. The result distinguishes a single-photon nonclassical signature from prior ensemble-level HHG effects such as squeezing or sub-Poissonian statistics.
main_result: The main theoretical result is predicted photon anti-bunching in HHG intensity correlation functions across the harmonic spectrum.
application_angle: If observed, the effect could establish HHG as a source of temporally nonclassical extreme-frequency photons.

title: Quantum mechanics in configuration space in context
abstract: To enhance the way in which wave-particle duality is implemented in the modelling of quantum mechanical systems, Bukhari et al. [New J. Phys. 27, 084501 (2025)] recently introduced an alternative approach to quantum mechanics, namely quantum mechanics in configuration space. This formalism is based on a physically motivated quantisation of Newtonian mechanics and promotes the classical position-velocity states (x,v) to pairwise distinguishable quantum states. The resulting |x,v> states form the basis of the Hilbert space of individual quantum mechanical particles and evolve along classical trajectories. In this paper, we consider the modelling of a mechanical particle in free space and put quantum mechanics in configuration space into context. It is shown that this formalism increases the continuity between quantum and classical mechanics by avoiding a conceptual inconsistency associated with the definition of momentum in canonical quantisation. In addition, we emphasise that standard quantum mechanics and quantum mechanics in configuration space are based on two distinct formulations of classical mechanics.
one_liner: The paper contextualizes a configuration-space formulation of quantum mechanics built from position-velocity states.
why_it_matters: Although not application hardware, it clarifies foundational modeling assumptions that can affect how quantum systems are represented.
detailed_summary: The paper examines quantum mechanics in configuration space, where classical position-velocity states |x,v> form distinguishable quantum basis states evolving along classical trajectories. It contrasts this framework with canonical quantization and argues that it improves continuity between classical and quantum mechanics by avoiding a momentum-definition inconsistency. The contribution is conceptual rather than a communication experiment or device demonstration.
main_result: The work situates the position-velocity-state formalism as a distinct quantization route from standard quantum mechanics.
application_angle: Its relevance is indirect, providing modeling perspective rather than an immediately deployable quantum-communication technology.

title: Principle of Entangled-Photon Thermometry for Ultrafast Laser Processing
abstract: A quantum-enhanced approach for fast temperature diagnostics in ultrashort laser surface processing is introduced. The goal is to overcome limitations of existing methods, such as plasma emission, emissivity changes during ablation, and the need for time-consuming pump-probe measurements. The proposed method exploits polarization anisotropy in entangled photon pairs. The idler photon interacts with the laser-affected material surface, while its entangled counterpart is detected in a remote optical arm. Temperature-dependent changes in the complex refractive index modify the reflectance of p- and s-polarizations on the idler path, altering the coincidence-resolved polarization statistics of the signal photons. Using a Qiskit-based model incorporating experimental pump-probe reflectometry data, remote reconstruction of rapid thermal dynamics during femtosecond laser ablation is demonstrated. Although based on simulation, the model employs literature data to represent realistic material behavior. Due to the discrete nature of single-photon events, classical sliding-window analysis suffers from shot noise and temporal inertia. To overcome this limitation, a multilayer perceptron (MLP) regression network is applied to extract implicit anisotropy information from the photon bitstream. Compared with the classical approach, the neural-network method improves reconstruction robustness, reduces temperature noise, enhances the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), and enables nanosecond-scale tracking of thermal dynamics. The results indicate that entangled-photon polarization anisotropy combined with machine-learning analysis is a promising approach for remote, background-rejected, high-speed temperature diagnostics in laser-matter interaction studies.
one_liner: Entangled-photon polarization correlations are proposed for nanosecond thermometry during femtosecond laser processing.
why_it_matters: Remote, background-rejected temperature readout could overcome emissivity, plasma, and pump-probe limits in laser-material diagnostics.
detailed_summary: The paper proposes using entangled photon pairs for temperature diagnostics during ultrafast laser surface processing. The idler reflects from the laser-affected surface, where temperature-dependent complex refractive-index changes alter p- and s-polarized reflectance, while the partner photon is analyzed through coincidence-resolved polarization statistics. A Qiskit-based simulation using literature pump-probe reflectometry data and an MLP regressor reconstructs rapid thermal dynamics with improved robustness, SNR, and nanosecond-scale tracking relative to sliding-window analysis.
main_result: Simulation shows remote reconstruction of laser-induced thermal dynamics from entangled-photon polarization bitstreams.
application_angle: The approach targets high-speed, low-background process monitoring for ultrafast laser machining and ablation studies.

title: Coherent Control of an Embedded Bound State Without a Spectral Gap
abstract: Bound states in the continuum (BICs) can confine photonic excitations in open systems without conventional cavities or band gaps, making them natural candidates for long-lived quantum storage and single-photon control. Their use is limited, however, by two obstacles: they are dark to incident photons, and they lack spectral-gap protection from the surrounding continuum. We overcome both limitations in a giant atom coupled to a one-dimensional waveguide using two temporal control knobs. Atomic-frequency modulation breaks and restores the destructive-interference condition, enabling deterministic capture and release of mode-matched single photons. Coupling modulation instead preserves the BIC condition while tuning the atomic and photonic weights of the stored state. A key result is that this embedded state can nevertheless be controlled adiabatically despite the absence of a spectral gap, with an intrinsic leakage probability linear in the ramp rate. By separating radiative access from BIC-preserving deformation, the protocol turns a dark BIC into a single-photon memory whose fidelity is set by the intrinsic continuum-induced leakage law, providing a route to embedded-state control in open photonic platforms.
one_liner: Temporal controls turn a dark waveguide BIC into a controllable single-photon memory without a spectral gap.
why_it_matters: It shows embedded photonic states can be accessed and adiabatically deformed despite continuum leakage constraints.
detailed_summary: The authors study a giant atom coupled to a one-dimensional waveguide supporting an embedded bound state without conventional gap protection. Atomic-frequency modulation breaks and restores the destructive-interference condition to capture and release mode-matched single photons, while coupling modulation tunes atomic versus photonic storage weight while preserving the BIC condition. They derive that adiabatic control remains possible without a spectral gap, with intrinsic leakage probability scaling linearly with ramp rate.
platform_or_system: giant atom in a one-dimensional waveguide
main_result: The protocol deterministically accesses and deforms an embedded BIC to realize a controllable single-photon memory.
application_angle: This offers a route to compact photonic quantum memories and single-photon control in open waveguide platforms.

title: Optimizing bias-tailored quantum error correction beyond code-capacity noise
abstract: We find that the substantial advantages predicted for bias-tailored quantum error correction (QEC) under code-capacity noise are strongly reduced once realistic syndrome extraction and circuit-level noise models are considered. We start by comparing XZZX codes to rectangular surface codes with a bias-dependent optimised anisotropy. Although code-capacity simulations predict an advantage of rectangular surface codes in the limit of high noise bias, this actually disappears under circuit-level noise, making the XZZX codes the preferred and simplest choice even for platforms that allow for a flexible variation of the code layout adapted to changes in noise calibration. Our results identify bias degradation during syndrome extraction under circuit-level noise as the central limitation of biased-tailored QEC. To partially mitigate this effect, we introduce a bias-filtering CNOT gadget that temporarily encodes the ancillary target qubit during syndrome extraction in a repetition code and, upon measurement and feed forward, manages to reduce the bias degradation. In a regime of high-bias and low-idle errors, this bias-filtering gadget yields a few-percent relative improvement of the XZZX code error threshold, demonstrating that lightweight bias-filtering strategies can recover part of the lost bias-tailoring advantage for realistic circuit-level noise.
one_liner: Circuit-level simulations show bias-tailored QEC loses much of its code-capacity advantage, with XZZX codes remaining the simplest strong choice.
why_it_matters: It identifies syndrome-extraction bias degradation as a practical bottleneck and tests a lightweight CNOT gadget that recovers part of the lost threshold gain.
detailed_summary: The paper compares XZZX codes against optimized rectangular surface codes under code-capacity and circuit-level biased-noise models. It finds that advantages predicted at code capacity largely vanish once noisy syndrome extraction is included, because the bias is degraded during the measurement circuit. A bias-filtering CNOT gadget temporarily repetition-encodes the ancilla target and uses measurement feedforward to reduce this degradation, giving a few-percent relative threshold improvement in high-bias, low-idle-error regimes.
main_result: Realistic circuit-level noise favors XZZX codes over bias-optimized rectangular layouts, while a bias-filtering CNOT recovers a small threshold improvement.
application_angle: The work informs hardware-aware QEC design for biased-noise platforms where syndrome-extraction circuits, not just data-qubit noise, set performance.

title: Quantum Routers: A Switching-Fabric Framework for Quantum-Native Forwarding
abstract: Forwarding in quantum networks cannot be realized by directly transposing classical switching fabrics, since the no-cloning theorem and the quantum measurement postulate constrain the direct relay of quantum information while ruling out copy-based buffering and inspection. In this paper, we propose a switching-fabric framework for quantum routers based on multipartite entanglement. Specifically, we formalize the notion of an entanglement-based switching fabric, in which a graph state acts as the forwarding resource and entanglement forwarding is realized through local Pauli measurements. We translate the classical notions of blocking and non-blocking operation into structural conditions for entanglement-based fabrics, by deriving the \textit{edge-controlled (EC) design principle} for non-blocking operation. We instantiate this principle through a monolithic \textit{EC crossbar} and a modular Clos-type EC fabric, for which we characterize resource scaling and identify the regime where the modular design becomes more resource-efficient than the monolithic one. Finally, a forwarding-latency analysis establishes a fundamental distinction between matching-oblivious and matching-driven forwarding: the proposed EC fabrics realize all requested input-output entanglement links with constant forwarding depth under sufficient measurement parallelism, whereas matching-driven EPR-based fabrics exhibit latency that scales with the number of requested connections. The proposed framework provides a hardware-agnostic foundation for quantum-router switching fabrics.
one_liner: A graph-state switching-fabric model enables quantum-router forwarding through Pauli measurements with constant-depth operation under parallelism.
why_it_matters: It translates router fabric concepts into quantum-native constraints and gives hardware-agnostic scaling rules for non-blocking quantum network nodes.
detailed_summary: The paper proposes entanglement-based switching fabrics where graph states provide the forwarding resource and local Pauli measurements establish input-output entanglement links. It derives an edge-controlled design principle for non-blocking operation and instantiates it with monolithic EC crossbar and modular Clos-type EC fabrics. The analysis compares resource scaling and shows that EC fabrics can realize all requested links in constant forwarding depth when measurements are sufficiently parallelized.
main_result: The framework defines non-blocking entanglement-switching fabrics and shows constant-depth forwarding for arbitrary requested matchings under parallel measurement.
application_angle: It provides architectural primitives for scalable quantum-network routers without relying on forbidden copy-based buffering or inspection.

title: Creating squeezed and non-classical collective motional many-body states through stroboscopic Rydberg dressing
abstract: Realizing conditional quantum operations, e.g., quantum gates, for quantum computing and simulation requires controlled interactions between particles. Often, these interactions depend on the interparticle distance, and accordingly, an uncertainty of the relative particle position may translate into gate infidelities. We consider here a quantum computing platform based on an array of neutral atoms and present a method that allows to reduce the uncertainty of all interatomic distances. Our approach exploits the coupling between atomic motion and stroboscopically excited atomic Rydberg states. It allows to collectively squeeze the modes corresponding to interatomic displacements, thereby reducing distance fluctuations down to a fraction of the motional vacuum state. Furthermore, the method permits the creation of non-classical states with substantial Wigner negativity. These correlated states may allow reducing motional decoherence, increasing gate fidelity, and potentially yield a resource for quantum-enhanced metrology.
one_liner: Stroboscopic Rydberg dressing is proposed to squeeze collective motional modes and suppress interatomic-distance fluctuations in neutral-atom arrays.
why_it_matters: Reducing distance uncertainty directly targets motional errors in Rydberg gates and can also generate non-classical motional resources for metrology.
detailed_summary: The work studies neutral-atom arrays where position uncertainty couples to distance-dependent Rydberg interactions and can reduce conditional-gate fidelity. By stroboscopically exciting Rydberg states, the scheme couples internal and motional dynamics to collectively squeeze interatomic-displacement modes below the motional vacuum level. The protocol also allows preparation of non-classical motional states with substantial Wigner negativity.
main_result: A stroboscopic Rydberg-dressing protocol can collectively squeeze interatomic-distance modes and create non-classical motional many-body states.
application_angle: The method could reduce motional decoherence in neutral-atom quantum processors and provide squeezed motional states for quantum-enhanced sensing.

title: Higher-order topological metasurface based on split-ring resonators with dipole-quadrupole couplings
abstract: Photonic higher-order topological insulators (HOTI) are characterized by a hierarchy of topologically-protected states with different dimensionalities, making them especially interesting for potential applications that combine strong localization of electromagnetic fields and their robust waveguiding. However, their practical implementation often requires expensive processing techniques and is limited by accessible material parameters. In this paper, we demonstrate that a radio-frequency photonic HOTI can be implemented as a metasurface composed of split-ring resonators with couplings between dipole and quadrupole modes. We verify, by numerical simulations and experimentally at frequencies of 1.5-1.7 GHz, that a proposed metasurface supports corner- and edge-localized states. Our results reveal a scalable and easily reconfigurable GHz-range platform that employs printed circuit board technology, thus making crucial steps required for further experimental studies of photonic HOTI and the development of their microwave applications.
one_liner: A PCB-compatible split-ring-resonator metasurface experimentally realizes GHz higher-order topological edge and corner states.
why_it_matters: It offers a low-cost, reconfigurable microwave HOTI platform for robust field localization, routing, and sensing-relevant metasurface devices.
detailed_summary: The paper demonstrates a radio-frequency photonic higher-order topological insulator using split-ring resonators with dipole-quadrupole couplings. Simulations and experiments at 1.5-1.7 GHz verify both edge-localized and corner-localized electromagnetic states. Because the structure is built using printed circuit board technology, it is scalable and experimentally reconfigurable compared with more demanding photonic implementations.
main_result: The authors numerically and experimentally demonstrate higher-order topological edge and corner modes in a 1.5-1.7 GHz PCB metasurface.
application_angle: Robust microwave localization and waveguiding could support compact RF photonic components, resonant sensors, and reconfigurable topological circuits.

title: Experimental Characterization and Modeling of Measurement-Induced State-Transitions in a Fluxonium Superconducting Qubit
abstract: Superconducting qubits are most often measured using dispersive readout, which, ideally, implements a projective quantum non-demolition (QND) measurement. While a larger readout drive can increase the signal and, thus, reduce discrimination errors in the readout, strong microwave drives may also cause non-QND errors by driving the qubit to a state outside the computational subspace. In this work, we experimentally characterize measurement-induced state transitions (MIST) in a fluxonium qubit over its full external flux range. We further numerically calculate the MIST errors, and find that the theory accurately predicts eleven experimentally identified regions with increased MIST. In addition to transitions to higher fluxonium levels, we also find that, at certain flux points, MIST errors are dominated by transitions that include the transmission-line-like array modes of the fluxonium's superinductor. The excellent match between theory and experiment validates that the models accurately predict the occurrence of MIST in these systems, and further highlights the influence of array modes in fluxonium readout.
one_liner: Fluxonium experiments map measurement-induced state transitions over flux bias and validate a model including higher levels and array modes.
why_it_matters: Accurate prediction of non-QND readout errors is essential for tuning fluxonium measurement strength without leaking out of the computational subspace.
detailed_summary: The study experimentally characterizes measurement-induced state transitions in a fluxonium qubit across its full external-flux range under dispersive readout. Numerical modeling accurately predicts eleven regions with elevated MIST error. Beyond transitions to higher fluxonium levels, the analysis finds flux points where transitions involving superinductor transmission-line-like array modes dominate the readout error.
main_result: Theory and experiment agree on flux-dependent regions of elevated measurement-induced transitions, including errors mediated by superinductor array modes.
application_angle: The results give a practical modeling tool for designing higher-fidelity fluxonium readout and avoiding flux/readout-drive settings that cause leakage.

title: Quantum Chip Paradigm Framework
abstract: Quantum Electronic Design Automation (Q-EDA) is emerging as quantum chips move from laboratory prototypes to scalable engineering systems. This paper argues that superconducting quantum chip design is approaching a "SPICE moment" similar to early classical EDA, where growing qubit scale, control complexity, frequency planning, packaging, process variation, and cryogenic measurement feedback require a shift from experience-based design to model-driven engineering. We propose a Quantum Chip Paradigm Framework that treats Q-EDA not only as software, but as part of the quantum chip development paradigm. Unlike classical HDL-first design, quantum chip design must begin with physical structures such as Josephson junctions, resonators, couplers, readout elements, control lines, and packaging environments. The framework emphasizes PCell-based modeling, SPICE-Q simulation, Quantum PDKs, and design-technology-measurement co-optimization. We further outline a hierarchical Q-EDA system spanning physical structures, qubit PCells, logical qubits, quantum arithmetic, functional quantum IP, and Quantum SoC systems. The key goal is to turn physical models, layout rules, simulation results, fabrication data, and measurement feedback into reusable and auditable engineering objects for large-scale quantum processors and fault-tolerant quantum computing.
one_liner: A Q-EDA framework argues for model-driven superconducting quantum-chip design from physical PCells through Quantum SoC abstractions.
why_it_matters: It frames scalable quantum processors as an engineering design-stack problem linking layouts, models, fabrication data, and cryogenic measurements.
detailed_summary: The paper proposes a Quantum Chip Paradigm Framework for superconducting quantum-chip design as systems grow beyond laboratory-scale prototypes. It emphasizes that quantum design must start from physical structures such as Josephson junctions, resonators, couplers, readout elements, control lines, and packaging, rather than an HDL-first abstraction. The framework organizes PCell-based modeling, SPICE-Q simulation, Quantum PDKs, and design-technology-measurement co-optimization into reusable engineering objects across physical, logical, IP, and system levels.
main_result: The paper defines a hierarchical Q-EDA framework connecting physical layouts, simulation, fabrication, measurement feedback, and reusable quantum-chip design objects.
application_angle: Such infrastructure could make large superconducting processors more auditable, manufacturable, and scalable by reducing reliance on ad hoc expert design.

title: SPICE-Q and Large-Scale Quantum Chip Production
abstract: We propose SPICE-Q, a SPICE-inspired design-technology co-optimization framework for superconducting quantum processors. Rather than replacing tools such as HFSS, Qiskit Metal, pyEPR, SQcircuit, SQuADDS, scqubits, or QuTiP, SPICE-Q aims to connect them through a unified, traceable data chain spanning process rules, layout, electromagnetic simulation, energy-participation-ratio and circuit quantization, Hamiltonian extraction, noise analysis, cryogenic test, and manufacturing feedback. The central mapping is from process and PDK constraints to layout geometry, electromagnetic modes, equivalent circuit parameters, effective Hamiltonians, and finally metrics such as frequency, coupling, anharmonicity, decoherence, readout performance, and yield. This flow must capture Josephson-junction variability, transmon frequency allocation, resonator and Purcell constraints, coupler crosstalk, microwave routing, 3D interconnects, material/interface loss, package modes, and wafer-scale process statistics. By introducing standardized model interfaces, statistical parameter models, model cards, version governance, and closed-loop calibration from cryogenic and fabrication data, SPICE-Q frames superconducting quantum-chip design as an engineering workflow rather than a collection of isolated simulations. We argue that scalable and fault-tolerant quantum processors will require such a continuous model chain from device physics and electromagnetic fields to quantum dynamics, noise, manufacturability, and system-level yield.
one_liner: SPICE-Q proposes a traceable multiphysics design flow for manufacturable superconducting quantum processors.
why_it_matters: It frames quantum-chip scaling as an engineering and yield problem linking PDK rules, EM simulation, Hamiltonians, noise, cryogenic test, and fab feedback.
detailed_summary: The paper proposes SPICE-Q as a SPICE-inspired design-technology co-optimization framework for superconducting quantum processors. It connects process rules, layout, EM simulation, EPR/circuit quantization, Hamiltonian extraction, noise analysis, test data, and manufacturing statistics into a unified data chain. The framework emphasizes standardized interfaces, statistical model cards, version governance, and closed-loop calibration from fabrication and cryogenic measurements.
main_result: The main contribution is an end-to-end workflow mapping process and PDK constraints to device metrics such as frequency, coupling, anharmonicity, decoherence, readout, and yield.
application_angle: A traceable model chain could reduce iteration cycles and support wafer-scale production of fault-tolerant superconducting quantum chips.

title: Twin-beam advantage in quantum LiDAR under correlated noise
abstract: Quantum light promises improved precision in optical remote sensing, but its practical advantage depends critically on whether nonclassical resources remain useful under realistic noise and experimentally accessible detection. This question becomes especially relevant for LiDAR systems, where a quantum advantage has been demonstrated for target detection and joint range-velocity estimation, but mostly under idealized conditions or simple noise models, such as optical loss and thermal background. A key open point is whether entanglement provides an operational advantage when the dominant disturbance is not independent noise, but structured interference across sensing modes. Here, we address this question by studying the joint estimation of target range and velocity with bright two-mode Gaussian probes and homodyne detection, comparing coherent, separable squeezed, and twin-beam states at a fixed resource budget. Our results reveal a hierarchy of quantum resources set by the noise structure: separable squeezing provides a robust advantage over coherent illumination under loss and thermal background, whereas twin-beam probes become superior under correlated jamming when the receiver is adaptively optimized. These results establish correlated noise as the operational regime in which entanglement provides a robustness advantage beyond local squeezing, opening a receiver-aware route to quantum-enhanced LiDAR in realistic and potentially adversarial environments.
one_liner: Twin-beam Gaussian probes can outperform local squeezing for quantum LiDAR when noise is correlated across modes.
why_it_matters: The work identifies correlated jamming as a practical regime where entanglement gives a receiver-aware robustness advantage in range-velocity estimation.
detailed_summary: The paper studies joint target range and velocity estimation using bright two-mode Gaussian probes with homodyne detection. It compares coherent, separable squeezed, and twin-beam states at fixed resources under loss, thermal background, and correlated jamming. Separable squeezing is robust under ordinary loss and thermal noise, while adaptively optimized twin-beam receivers become superior under structured correlated interference.
main_result: The main result is a resource hierarchy showing entanglement becomes operationally advantageous under correlated jamming rather than independent noise alone.
application_angle: This points toward quantum-enhanced LiDAR designs for realistic or adversarial environments where interference is structured across sensing modes.

title: Fabless Quantum Chip Design and Commercial Production
abstract: This paper proposes a fabless quantum-chip design and production architecture for superconducting quantum computing, centered on the SPICE-Q multiphysics simulation framework. The proposed ecosystem connects process-certified quantum PDKs, parameterized device cells, traceable model cards, SPICE-Q physical modeling languages, unified Q-EDA flows, foundry sign-off rules, cryogenic test feedback, and reusable quantum IP. In this model, design firms do not merely outsource fabrication; they prepare verified tape-outs under standardized process constraints and calibrated physical models. Its economic value lies in reducing repetitive device debugging, process exploration, and low-level layout effort, while its feasibility depends on PDK maturity, foundry yield, cryogenic test throughput, model-prediction accuracy, data-feedback mechanisms, and IP licensing boundaries. We argue that superconducting quantum chips can move from the current largely vertically integrated development model toward a fabless-foundry ecosystem only when hardware design is supported by standardized, verifiable, and reusable software and process interfaces. The required pillars are certified PDKs, PCell-based parameterized design, SPICE-Q cross-physics simulation, end-to-end Q-EDA automation, and a tradable quantum-IP market. By adapting lessons from the classical semiconductor industry to quantum hardware, this framework defines a path toward scalable, manufacturable, and commercially reusable superconducting quantum-chip design.
one_liner: A fabless superconducting quantum-chip ecosystem is proposed around certified PDKs, Q-EDA, reusable IP, and SPICE-Q modeling.
why_it_matters: It translates classical semiconductor fabless-foundry practices into quantum hardware, targeting manufacturability, reuse, and commercial scale-up.
detailed_summary: The paper outlines a fabless design and production architecture for superconducting quantum computing centered on SPICE-Q. It links process-certified quantum PDKs, parameterized cells, traceable model cards, foundry sign-off, cryogenic test feedback, and reusable quantum IP. The proposal argues that standardized, verifiable software and process interfaces are prerequisites for moving beyond vertically integrated quantum-chip development.
main_result: The main result is a proposed commercial architecture for verified quantum tape-outs using PDKs, PCells, SPICE-Q cross-physics simulation, Q-EDA automation, and IP reuse.
application_angle: A fabless model could lower design cost, improve foundry utilization, and accelerate production of reusable superconducting quantum hardware blocks.

title: Correlating Quasi-Optical Coupling Efficiency with Measured Receiver Noise Temperature in Metalens Coupled THz HEB Mixer
abstract: Quasi-optical coupling serves as the critical interface in terahertz (THz) heterodyne receiver systems, enabling efficient transfer of incident radiation to superconducting hot-electron bolometer (HEB) mixers through a focusing element and a planar microwave antenna. With recent advances in nanofabrication, planar dielectric metalenses have emerged as promising alternatives to conventional refractive optics due to their compactness and scalability. However, unlike conventional elliptical silicon lenses that are often treated as nearly ideal optical components, the focusing efficiency of metalenses is strongly dependent on the local deflection angle across the aperture, creating an urgent need to quantitatively understand the coupling between a dielectric metalens and a planar antenna. In this work, we present a quasi-optical coupling analysis between a planar Si metalens and a logarithmic spiral antenna integrated with a THz superconducting NbN HEB mixer operating at 1.63 THz using a spherical-coordinate vectorial integration. By combining the angular radiation profile of the spiral antenna with the deflection-angle-dependent focusing efficiency of the metalens obtained from numerical simulations, the calculated coupling efficiency is directly correlated with experimentally measured double-side-band receiver noise temperatures through comparison with a conventional elliptical Si lens measured under the same receiver configuration. The analysis establishes a quantitative relationship between metalens focusing efficiency, antenna coupling, and receiver noise temperature, providing guidance for optimizing metalens design and improving the overall performance of metalens-integrated THz heterodyne receivers.
one_liner: Metalens-to-antenna coupling is quantitatively tied to receiver noise in a 1.63 THz superconducting HEB mixer.
why_it_matters: The work gives an experimental design metric for compact metalens-integrated THz heterodyne receivers, not just simulated focusing efficiency.
detailed_summary: The paper analyzes quasi-optical coupling between a planar silicon metalens and a logarithmic spiral antenna integrated with a superconducting NbN hot-electron bolometer mixer at 1.63 THz. It combines the antenna radiation profile with deflection-angle-dependent metalens focusing efficiency from simulations using spherical-coordinate vectorial integration. The calculated coupling is correlated with measured double-sideband receiver noise temperatures and compared against an elliptical silicon lens under the same receiver setup.
main_result: The main result is a quantitative correlation between metalens focusing efficiency, antenna coupling, and measured receiver noise temperature.
application_angle: The method guides compact THz heterodyne receiver optimization for spectroscopy, astronomy, security imaging, and high-frequency sensing.

title: Kerr Soliton Generation in Ultra-Compact Photonic Devices
abstract: Chip-based nonlinear photonics offer the capability to integrate devices with all the requisite photonic components (e.g., filters, couplers, detectors) into highly compact form factors. This offers the possibility of making the devices scalable, robust, and manufacturable. Such integrated photonic devices will enable applications in optical communications, precision metrology, microwave generation, and LIDAR. However, thermal instabilities represent a major hurdle in the deterministic operation of nonlinear optical processes in such integrated resonant structures such as microresonators. In this work we demonstrate deterministic and highly stable Kerr soliton comb generation in tight-spiral microresonators with comb spacings as low as 16 GHz. We perform a comprehensive experimentally-validated thermal model of such compact microresonators and reveal non-trivial thermally-driven instabilities governing the cavity soliton dynamics. We design and implement a fast feedback loop on the devices to overcome thermal perturbations and stabilize cavity-soliton states, including those that are otherwise unstable, and to allow for controlled transitions between the soliton states. Our approach enables the realization of thermally-stable highly compact soliton microcomb devices in a wide variety of photonic platforms.
one_liner: Fast feedback stabilizes deterministic Kerr soliton combs in ultra-compact tight-spiral microresonators down to 16 GHz spacing.
why_it_matters: Stable low-spacing soliton microcombs in compact chips are key for deployable optical communications, metrology, microwave synthesis, and LiDAR.
detailed_summary: The paper demonstrates deterministic and highly stable Kerr soliton comb generation in tight-spiral microresonators with comb spacings as low as 16 GHz. An experimentally validated thermal model identifies nontrivial thermally driven instabilities that affect soliton dynamics in compact resonators. The authors implement a fast feedback loop that suppresses thermal perturbations, stabilizes otherwise unstable soliton states, and controls transitions between states.
main_result: The main result is stable, deterministic Kerr soliton generation in ultra-compact microresonators using feedback control of thermal dynamics.
application_angle: The approach supports manufacturable chip-scale comb sources for optical links, precision metrology, microwave generation, and LiDAR systems.

title: Singular Vector Finite Element Basis Functions for Tetrahedra in Complex Electromagnetic Geometries
abstract: Electromagnetic finite element method (FEM) implementations using traditional basis functions struggle to accurately represent field behavior near singular features such as conducting wedges. To combat this, specialized singular basis functions have been introduced to directly model the singular fields in these regions, leading to substantially improved performance. While these efforts have been pursued extensively in 2D, few functions have been developed for 3D elements. In this work, we develop basis functions for this in tetrahedra. Unlike prior functions, these basis functions are additive, meaning they are included alongside the standard vector basis functions to achieve more robust performance. Further, these functions are designed to be adaptable to tetrahedra touching several unique singular features by using combinations of basis functions singular with respect to each node and edge in the element, making them applicable to highly complex geometries. Higher-order interpolatory versions of the basis functions for modeling singular behavior with greater accuracy are also provided. These basis functions lead to substantial improvements in accuracy relative to the standard basis functions, and allow otherwise expensive simulations to be performed at far lower costs. As an application example, we perform simulations to extract critical quantities for designing superconducting qubits that significantly depend on the behavior of singular fields. In Ansys HFSS, this took 21.27 hours and a peak memory usage of 6.23 TB with 800 processors available, while using our singular basis functions achieved comparable results in 196 seconds while using 27.24 GB of memory and only 16 processors. Due to these benefits, our singular basis functions could be applied to enable design optimization of electromagnetic geometries with dominantly singular behavior, such as superconducting qubits.
one_liner: Additive singular vector FEM basis functions make 3D EM simulations of sharp superconducting-qubit geometries vastly cheaper.
why_it_matters: Accurate singular-field modeling can speed qubit layout optimization and reduce the computational burden of extracting loss-sensitive EM parameters.
detailed_summary: The paper develops additive singular vector basis functions for tetrahedral finite elements in 3D electromagnetic simulations. These functions can be combined with standard vector bases and adapted to tetrahedra touching multiple singular nodes or edges, including higher-order interpolatory variants. In a superconducting-qubit design example, comparable results to a very large HFSS run are reported with far lower runtime, memory, and processor count.
platform_or_system: 3D FEM electromagnetic simulation for superconducting qubits
main_result: The main result is comparable singular-field-sensitive qubit EM extraction in 196 seconds using 27.24 GB and 16 processors versus 21.27 hours and 6.23 TB in HFSS.
application_angle: This could make iterative optimization of qubit geometries with wedges, edges, and interface-loss-sensitive fields much more practical.

title: Optimal Calibration of Quantum Network Links
abstract: The reliable distribution of entanglement is essential for the effective operation of quantum networks. Due to fundamental differences between quantum and classical communication systems, it is necessary to develop specialised algorithms and protocols that also account for quantum-specific constraints. In this work, we focus on the issue of recalibration. As suggested by recent experimental studies, the process of local entanglement generation in a quantum link degrades over time due to environmental changes that have to be estimated and compensated via a calibration operation, during which the link is not available. Therefore, in such a quantum network, every link alternates between an activation period, during which it operates normally, and a calibration period, during which it cannot participate in the end-to-end entanglement distribution, thereby creating a trade-off between link quality (the fidelity of generated pairs, which decays during activation) and availability (the fraction of time the link is usable, which calibration reduces). We develop analytically a protocol for optimally assigning activation periods to each link in linear quantum repeater chains, subject to any general end-to-end fidelity requirements and local initial fidelity thresholds. Building on this foundation, we extend to general quantum networks, where multiple paths may cross at common links, proposing a heuristic approach evaluated in simulations and compared with a benchmark, numerical approach, and theoretical bounds.
one_liner: Optimizes when quantum-network links should recalibrate as entanglement fidelity decays during operation.
why_it_matters: Turns an experimental reliability issue in repeater networks into schedulable control logic balancing link quality against uptime.
detailed_summary: The paper models quantum links whose locally generated entanglement degrades during activation and is restored by calibration downtime. It derives optimal activation-period assignments for linear repeater chains under end-to-end fidelity constraints and local initial-fidelity thresholds. For general networks with shared links and multiple paths, it proposes a heuristic and benchmarks it against numerical methods and theoretical bounds.
main_result: The main result is an analytically optimal recalibration protocol for linear repeater chains plus a scalable heuristic for general quantum networks.
application_angle: The work is directly relevant to operating future quantum networks where calibration downtime and pair fidelity must be managed automatically.

title: Learning Arbitrary Lindbladians with Quantum Error Correction
abstract: We study ansatz-free Lindbladian learning, the problem of reconstructing the generator of an open quantum system without prior knowledge of its Hamiltonian or dissipator structures. This problem exhibits two distinct information-theoretic precision limits: Hamiltonian components unmasked by dissipation are Heisenberg-limited, while the remaining Lindbladian components are subject to the quadratically worse standard quantum limit. Existing approaches that attain these optimal scalings strongly rely on pre-specified structure of interaction and noise, leaving the ansatz-free setting an open problem. In this work, we present the first standard-quantum-limited algorithm for learning arbitrary sparse Lindbladians. Under an additional physically motivated regularity condition, our framework also learns the Hamiltonian component disjoint from the dissipator at the Heisenberg limit, without prior knowledge of either the Hamiltonian or dissipator supports. Our main technical ingredient is a recursive random stabilizer-code construction that suppresses the strongest Lindbladian terms while preserving sensitivity to weaker unknown ones. These results establish a scalable framework for characterizing unknown open quantum systems, with quantum error correction serving as a key learning primitive.
one_liner: Uses recursive stabilizer-code ideas to learn sparse unknown Lindbladian dynamics without an ansatz.
why_it_matters: Provides a route to scalable characterization of open quantum hardware when both coherent interactions and noise channels are unknown.
detailed_summary: The paper tackles ansatz-free reconstruction of arbitrary sparse Lindbladian generators, covering both Hamiltonian and dissipative components. Its key tool is a recursive random stabilizer-code construction that suppresses dominant Lindbladian terms while maintaining sensitivity to weaker unknown terms. The framework reaches standard-quantum-limit scaling generally and, under a regularity condition, Heisenberg scaling for Hamiltonian components disjoint from dissipation.
platform_or_system: open quantum systems with QEC probes
main_result: The main result is the first standard-quantum-limited algorithm for learning arbitrary sparse Lindbladians without knowing Hamiltonian or dissipator supports.
application_angle: It can support diagnostic protocols for quantum processors and sensors where noise mechanisms are complex and not known in advance.

title: Einstein-Podolsky-Rosen correlations between mechanical oscillators revealed through SU(1,1) interferometry
abstract: Quantum correlations are essential for achieving quantum advantage in computing, communication and sensing. Moreover, their observation challenges and constrains our fundamental understanding of nature. Mechanical oscillators in the quantum regime provide an appealing platform for preparing and investigating quantum correlations at macroscopic scales. Despite substantial progress, however, continuous-variable quantum correlations stronger than entanglement have not yet been observed in this macroscopic regime. Here, we report the experimental observation of continuous-variable Einstein-Podolsky-Rosen correlations between two spatially-separated mechanical oscillators with an effective mass of $\sim 16 \,μg$ each. This is achieved by coupling them to a superconducting qubit which allows for engineering a two-mode squeezing interaction when parametrically driven. Crucially, we show that this interaction can be used to witness quantum correlations through the realization of a mechanical SU(1,1) interferometer. Our results expand the toolbox of operations in circuit quantum acoustodynamics and demonstrate that quantum correlations stronger than entanglement can also be observed in macroscopic systems, thereby shedding light on the boundary between quantum and classical regimes.
one_liner: Reports EPR correlations between two microgram-scale mechanical oscillators using a qubit-mediated SU(1,1) interferometer.
why_it_matters: Pushes continuous-variable quantum correlations stronger than entanglement into a macroscopic mechanical platform.
detailed_summary: The experiment couples two spatially separated mechanical oscillators, each with effective mass around 16 micrograms, to a superconducting qubit. Parametric driving engineers a two-mode squeezing interaction that creates continuous-variable EPR correlations. The same interaction is used to realize a mechanical SU(1,1) interferometer that witnesses the correlations.
main_result: The main result is experimental observation of continuous-variable EPR correlations between two spatially separated macroscopic mechanical oscillators.
application_angle: The capability expands circuit quantum acoustodynamics tools for macroscopic quantum tests and mechanically mediated quantum sensing or information processing.

title: Impulse Decoding of Quantum LDPC Codes: Equivalence of Degeneracy and Code-Shortening
abstract: Quantum error correction is essential for building scalable quantum computers. Within the stabilizer formalism, the Calderbank-Shor-Steane framework constructs quantum codes from pairs of classical linear codes. A distinctive feature in this setting is degeneracy, where multiple equivalent error estimates exist-a phenomenon that has no classical counterpart, and the lack of a meaningful classical coding-theoretic interpretation of which has remained a gap in the literature. In this paper, we demonstrate that degeneracy is closely related to the classical operation of shortening of a linear block code. Interestingly, the shortening here takes place at the decoder rather than at the encoder. Leveraging this insight, we present a parallel decoding scheme for quantum low-density parity-check codes, which we term impulse decoding, that significantly outperforms belief propagation with ordered statistics decoding, as well as several other existing techniques, under both code-capacity and circuit-level noise, with significantly lesser complexity. We then present another algorithm based on decoding of residual errors, which when combined with impulse decoding achieves further performance improvement under circuit-level noise.
one_liner: Recasts quantum-code degeneracy as decoder-side code shortening and builds a lower-complexity LDPC decoder.
why_it_matters: Improved practical decoding is a key bottleneck for scalable fault-tolerant quantum hardware.
detailed_summary: The paper gives a classical coding-theoretic interpretation of stabilizer-code degeneracy by relating it to shortening performed at the decoder. Using this view, it introduces impulse decoding, a parallel decoding scheme for quantum LDPC codes. Simulations show performance gains over belief propagation with ordered statistics decoding and other methods under code-capacity and circuit-level noise, with lower complexity; a residual-error decoder further improves circuit-level performance.
main_result: The main result is a parallel impulse decoder that outperforms several existing quantum LDPC decoding techniques at reduced complexity.
application_angle: Lower-complexity, higher-performance LDPC decoding could improve real-time error correction for large-scale quantum computers.

title: All Reflective Field-widened Unbalanced Interferometer for Quantum Sensing and Communication Applications
abstract: Interference of time-bin encoded signals over free-space optical channels typically requires stringent mode filtering on receivers due to wavefront distortions from atmospheric turbulence, conventionally addressed with adaptive optics. Passive multimode receivers based on field-widened interferometers present a compelling alternative, enabling direct interference without the overhead of wavefront correction. We demonstrate a field-widened interferometer design that is implemented solely with reflective surfaces and achieves a high interference visibility (greater than 0.97) for spatially multimode beams. The interference of the multimode beams is enabled by two imaging systems that consist of a cavity configuration between a spherical concave mirror and a flat mirror. The configuration enables small form-factors, is inherently achromatic, and is based on standard spherical mirrors which reduces the complexity of the system. The interferometer is applicable for spatially multimode and turbulent optical channels, such as satellite communication, and is designed for quantum systems that use time-bin encoded qubits.
one_liner: Demonstrates an all-reflective field-widened interferometer with >0.97 visibility for spatially multimode beams.
why_it_matters: Offers a passive receiver architecture for time-bin quantum links through multimode or turbulent free-space channels.
detailed_summary: The work presents an unbalanced interferometer made solely from reflective surfaces for interference of time-bin encoded signals. Two imaging systems based on spherical concave and flat mirror cavities enable high-visibility interference for spatially multimode beams. The reflective design is compact, achromatic, and uses standard spherical mirrors, reducing system complexity compared with adaptive-optics-heavy receivers.
